metadata.title: Why Realistic Games Hook Players: The Physics of Belief

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Two games with similar production cost can hook players very differently depending on the kind of realism they offer. Perceptual realism, how convincing the world looks and feels, supports premium pricing. These realism layers are not replayability features; they work on belief rather than repetition. Social realism is the highest-variance layer, producing the widest range of player responses.

artifactLines: Two games with similar production cost can hook players very differently depending on the kind of realism they offer.
Perceptual realism, how convincing the world looks and feels, supports premium pricing.
These realism layers are not replayability features; they work on belief rather than repetition.
Social realism is the highest-variance layer, producing the widest range of player responses.

beats.narration_text: The layered claim that perceptual realism supports premium pricing while social realism is the highest-variance layer, and that these are not replayability features, reframes how I see game value. I want to test it against real titles. Can you (1) define perceptual realism versus social realism with a concrete example of each, (2) explain why social realism has the highest variance in player outcomes, and (3) compare two similarly-priced games and predict which realism layer actually justifies their premium?
